title: Wax Crayon on Carpet
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; NO iron on synthetic carpet
stainChemistry: Same chemistry as crayon on upholstery: paraffin wax + concentrated pigment (iron oxides for earth tones, organic dyes for brights). Crayon deposits on carpet fiber as a physical wax object + leaves separate pigment behind after wax removal. Two bad-advice targets on carpet specifically: (1) the "iron + brown paper bag" trick works on cotton fabric but is DANGEROUS on synthetic carpet — nylon / polyester / olefin can soften, distort, or melt under a hot iron, and olefin (Berber, indoor-outdoor) is the most vulnerable. (2) The oily-DIY family (peanut butter, WD-40, mayo) softens wax but leaves a grease halo that's harder to clean than the crayon on fabric that can't be flushed. Clean answer: freeze the wax brittle, crack it off, citrus solvent for residue, separate pigment treatment. Works on all fibers without the iron risk.
whyThisWorks: Freezing (ice bag) hardens paraffin brittle so bulk lifts off mechanically — safe on every fiber. Goo Gone dissolves remaining wax film. Separate peroxide pass handles residual pigment. Freeze-and-solvent is the universal-safe path that doesn't depend on fiber identification.
defaultAssumption: Kid coloring on the living-room or bedroom rug. Most home carpet is synthetic — NO IRON. Wool carpet is rare and tolerates careful low heat through a barrier, but most home carpet is not wool. Default to freeze-and-solvent; skip heat entirely when fiber is unknown.

spottingProtocol.agent: Fiber ID — NO IRON on Synthetic
spottingProtocol.equipment: Carpet tag if available, good light
spottingProtocol.instruction: Synthetic carpet (nylon, polyester, olefin, triexta) = NO IRON. Wool = iron OK through heavy barrier, careful. Unknown = NO IRON, freeze-and-solvent only. Olefin softens at modest heat. Most home carpet is synthetic.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Ice in sealed plastic bag, plastic card or dull knife
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press a sealed bag of ice on the crayon for 10–15 min. Never direct ice on carpet — water damage. Wax gets brittle.
Crack the frozen wax with the edge of a plastic card. Lift off bulk pieces. Re-freeze if it softens. Don't gouge fibers.

spottingProtocol.agent: Goo Gone on a White Cloth
spottingProtocol.equipment: Goo Gone on white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Goo Gone to a cloth (never pour on carpet). Tamp from top, outside-in. Flip cloth as it picks up wax. Ventilate — flammable. 2–3 passes typical.
Dawn in cool water on damp cloth. Soft toothbrush. Blot. Damp-cloth rinse, never pour.

spottingProtocol.technique: Separate dye treatment after wax is gone
spottingProtocol.instruction: Once wax is gone, pigment may remain. Hidden-area test first. 3% peroxide on damp cloth 5 min. Blot. Dark crayons (red / purple / black) may need 2 rounds.
Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan-dry. Inspect in daylight.

steps: 1. Fiber ID: synthetic = NO iron. Unknown = no iron.
2. Freeze with ice bag 10–15 min. Crack + lift bulk.
3. Goo Gone tamp from top, outside-in.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold (freeze) + ambient solvent; no iron on synthetic
warnings: NEVER iron on synthetic carpet (nylon / polyester / olefin / triexta)
NEVER iron on unknown fiber — default freeze + solvent
NEVER peanut butter / WD-40 / mayo — grease halo
Pigment is a separate stain after wax is gone

homeSolutions.agent: Don't Iron Synthetic Carpet — And Don't Use Peanut Butter
homeSolutions.instruction: Two bad-advice traps to avoid. (1) The viral "iron + brown paper bag" trick works on COTTON FABRIC but NOT on most carpet. Most home carpet is synthetic (nylon, polyester, olefin, triexta) and can soften, melt, or distort under a hot iron. Olefin (a lot of Berber and indoor-outdoor carpet) is especially heat-sensitive. (2) "Peanut butter / WD-40 / mayo" tricks soften crayon wax but leave a grease stain on carpet that you can't flush like you can flush fabric. Clean answer: freeze the wax. Safe on every fiber, no grease halo.
Put ice in a zip-lock bag and press directly on the crayon for 10–15 minutes. NEVER put ice directly on carpet — water damage. The wax will get hard and brittle. You can feel it change under the bag.

Once frozen, crack the wax with the edge of a plastic card, old credit card, or dull butter knife. Lift the pieces out of the pile. Most of the bulk comes off cleanly this way. If it starts softening, refreeze. Don't gouge the fibers with sharp edges.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone or Citrus Solvent on a White Rag
homeSolutions.instruction: A thin wax film usually remains in the pile. Put Goo Gone (orange bottle, hardware / grocery store) on a clean white rag — NEVER pour on carpet. Press and tamp from the TOP. Flip the rag as it picks up wax and pigment. Work outside-in. Open windows — Goo Gone is flammable, no flame, no smoking.
Work a drop of Dawn with cool water into a damp cloth, press on the spot with a soft toothbrush. Lifts residual wax + solvent residue. Damp-cloth rinse — don't pour water on the carpet.

homeSolutions.agent: If Color Remains — Separate Dye Treatment
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the wax is gone, you may still see the crayon color in the carpet fiber (especially red, blue, purple, or black). That's now a dye stain, not a wax stain. Hidden-area test first. Dab 3% hydrogen peroxide on a damp white cloth, press 5 minutes, blot. Dark crayon colors (red, purple, black) may need 2 rounds.
Stack dry paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, 30 minutes. Fan-dry until the carpet is fully dry. Inspect in daylight.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Melted-In or Olefin / Wool / Stubborn
homeSolutions.instruction: Pro if: someone already ironed the spot and melted wax into the fiber, the carpet is wool (pro-only), the carpet is a Berber / indoor-outdoor (often olefin with solvent sensitivity), or color persists after 2 rounds on pigment. Tell them: "Crayon on [nylon / polyester / olefin / wool / unknown] carpet, age [fresh / melted-in], home-treated with freeze + Goo Gone + Dawn + [3% peroxide]. No iron attempted / iron was attempted with X result. [Residual pigment / melted-in / fiber concern]." Don't mention peanut butter even if someone tried it earlier.

steps: 1. Fiber ID — synthetic or unknown = NO IRON.
2. NO peanut butter / WD-40 / mayo — grease halo.
3. Freeze with ice-in-bag 10–15 min. Crack + lift.
4. Goo Gone on rag, tamp from top, outside-in. Ventilate.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if someone already ironed the spot (melted wax into fiber), if the carpet is olefin / Berber with solvent sensitivity, if the carpet is wool, or if pigment persists after 2 peroxide rounds on fiber-tested carpet.
warnings: NEVER iron synthetic carpet — melts fiber
NEVER iron unknown fiber — default freeze + solvent
NEVER peanut butter / WD-40 / mayo / hair conditioner — grease halo
Never direct ice on carpet — water damage
Never pour solvent or water on carpet
Pigment is a separate stain AFTER wax is gone

neverDo: Never iron on synthetic or unknown carpet fiber
Never oily DIY tricks — grease halo on carpet
Never use solvent near open flame
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / triexta carpet: freeze + solvent OK. NO IRON.
Olefin (polypropylene): freeze + solvent OK; NO IRON — melting risk
Wool: careful iron through heavy barrier possible; pro for tight pigment
Unknown fiber: treat as synthetic — no heat

materialWarnings: Iron method DANGEROUS on synthetic carpet
Oily DIY hacks leave grease halo
Pigment is a separate stain after wax

escalation.when: Iron was already used and wax is melted in, olefin / Berber / indoor-outdoor carpet with solvent sensitivity, wool carpet with tight pigment, or residual color after 2 peroxide rounds on fiber-tested carpet.
escalation.whatToTell: "Crayon on [nylon / polyester / olefin / wool / unknown] carpet, age [fresh / melted-in], home-treated with freeze + Goo Gone + Dawn + [3% peroxide]. No iron attempted [/ iron was attempted with X result]. [Residual pigment / melted-in / fiber concern]. No oily DIY tricks used."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with solvent spotting + fiber ID

contextQuestions: Crayon color — dark (red / purple / black) or light?
Fresh or melted-in from warm room / sat on?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Ironing synthetic carpet is the most dangerous piece of widespread advice for this stain. Olefin carpet is especially heat-sensitive. Dark crayons leave more pigment residue — budget extra peroxide rounds.
